text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025

children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

14.1	The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) is responsible for leading and coordinating action on environmental matters within the United Nations system. The mandate derives primarily from the priorities established in General Assembly resolution 2997 (XXVII), decision 19/1 of the Governing Council of UNEP and the Nairobi Declaration on the Role and Mandate of the United Nations Environment Programme, which the Assembly endorsed in 1997 in the annex to its resolution S-19/2 and further reaffirmed in its resolutions 53/242, in 1999, and 66/288 and 67/213, in 2012. In Assembly resolution 70/1, Member States recognized the importance of the environmental dimension to the successful implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. Within its mandate, UNEP supports normative processes, frameworks and tools and regional and country actions that demonstrate and promote the coherent implementation of the environmental dimension of sustainable development.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

14.2	Delivery of the 2027 programme plan is part of the effort to implement the UNEP medium-term strategy for the period 2026–2029. By leveraging science, strengthening environmental governance and encouraging global cooperation, UNEP aims to tackle environmental challenges and support coordinated and impactful action. Its three thematic subprogrammes (climate action, nature action and chemicals and pollution action) will drive the medium-term strategy vision, while the two foundational subprogrammes (subprogramme 4, Environmental law and governance, and subprogramme 7, Science-policy) and two enabling subprogrammes (subprogramme 2, Digital transformations in supporting environmental action, and subprogramme 6, Finance and economic transformations) will, respectively, deliver the vision – through science, environmental governance, finance and economic instruments and digital tools and platforms – to enhance international cooperation, strengthen capacity-building and share good practices.

14.3	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, subregional, national and local levels, UNEP will continue to raise ambitions and promote leadership through advocacy and a normative and standard-setting approach. It will support Member States through transformative strategic partnerships that deliver sustainable solutions, integrating health, education, poverty reduction, and economic and social well-being. Its partnerships with the secretariats of multilateral environmental agreements will remain pivotal to securing stronger synergies and enhancing the impact of the work of the relevant parties. UNEP will encourage non-governmental stakeholders, including the private sector and the finance community, to increase investment in new and renewable sources of energy and improve environmental sustainability. Within the context of those partnerships, action will be aimed at making a broader impact in support of sustainable development, while strengthening the ability of countries to rapidly respond to the environmental impacts of disasters and armed conflicts.

14.4	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UNEP, as Chair of the United Nations Environment Management Group, will continue to support and enhance coordination, cooperation and collaboration across the United Nations through system-wide strategies and common approaches on the environment, such as the common approach to biodiversity and the common approach towards a pollution-free planet. In addition, UNEP will help integrate the priorities of multilateral environmental agreements into United Nations country programming, ensuring a more coordinated and effective response to specific country needs. It will also enhance its guiding role, raise ambitions and accelerate and scale up progress to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals. It will continue to focus on ensuring that its work is reflected and embedded in the new generation of United Nations country teams and the reinvigorated resident coordinator system through engagement with the Development Coordination Office and to leverage its work and expertise at the regional, subregional and country levels. UNEP will also integrate the priorities of the multilateral environmental agreements into global follow-up and review processes, in particular the 2030 Agenda.
14.5	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	Member States and partners remain committed to the development and implementation of science-based environmental policies;
(b)	The continued availability of extrabudgetary funding for the implementation of UNEP planned activities.

14.6	UNEP integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. UNEP will continue to support Member States in mainstreaming gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls, utilizing disaggregated data and calling for women’s meaningful participation at all levels of environmental decision-making.

14.7	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, UNEP will continue to integrate disability inclusion and related guidance into its strategic, policy, programmatic and operational interventions. Engagement with organizations of persons with disabilities will be institutionalized to ensure a participatory approach to the implementation of the UNEP programme of work. Following lessons learned from the seventh session of the United Nations Environment Assembly, UNEP will establish structured mechanisms to assess accessibility needs in advance of major meetings and will conduct accessibility audits of digital platforms in line with the standards set out in the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

14.8	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planning deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

14.9	UNEP will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions taken in connection with a thorough assessment – led by UNEP and the secretariat of the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change – of current arrangements for environmental responsibilities which are dispersed across United Nations entities with a view to making proposals on possible structural changes and programme realignments on environmental issues, as appropriate.

children.text: Evaluation activities
14.12	The following evaluations completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	Office of Internal Oversight Services thematic evaluation of Sustainable Development Goal climate-focused partnerships;
(b)	UNEP evaluation of subprogramme 5, Chemicals and pollution action, during the period 2015-2024.

14.13	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, UNEP will seek to develop tailored partnership strategies that incorporate Secretariat-level guidance, ensuring clear operational guidelines on private sector engagement. This will help establish partnerships that are both purpose-driven and effective and will create clear road maps, including measurable targets that align with globally recognized frameworks, such as the Paris Agreement. UNEP will also increase efforts towards elevating the visibility and strategic importance of sound management of chemicals and waste and pollution prevention and control, across the United Nations system, especially among the United Nations country teams.
14.14	An evaluation of subprogramme 7, conducted by UNEP, is planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Climate action
Objective

14.15	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the transition of countries to low-emission economic development and increase their adaptation and resilience to climate change.
Strategy
14.16	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Support Member States with the most up-to-date science to underpin national and international policy-setting;

(b)	Provide guidance and technical assistance on climate plans, sectoral road maps, transparency and technology transfer to support Member States in fulfilling their climate obligations under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change and the Paris Agreement;
(c)	Accelerate sectoral transformation models to help Member States with low-emission development and increased adaptation and resilience;
(d)	Build the technical capacity of Member States and subnational governments to scale sectoral solutions through private sector partnerships and innovative climate finance.
14.17	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goal 13.
14.18	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Member States translating science and policy recommendations into actionable and investable climate plans that strengthen sustainable development and increasing their capacity to monitor progress transparently and efficiently;
(b)	Member States lowering greenhouse gas emissions by implementing sectoral mitigation solutions, including in the energy, transport, cooling, construction and land use sectors;

(c)	Member States enhancing resilience and adaptation to the impacts of climate change, including through nature-based solutions and improved multi-hazard monitoring, forecasting and early warning systems;

(d)	Member States using climate strategies (e.g. nationally determined contributions, national adaptation plans and biennial transparency reviews) to guide investment and attract innovative, concessional and private finance to accelerate mitigation, adaptation and resilience.
Programme performance in 2025
Cities commit to sustainable cooling targets

14.19	In its Global Cooling Watch report for 2025, entitled The Free Degrees: How Sustainable, Passive-First Cooling Can Save Lives, Money and Food, UNEP warns that cooling demand could more than triple by 2050, reaching 10 per cent of global emissions. In the report, UNEP outlines a sustainable cooling pathway, combining passive cooling, higher energy efficiency standards and accelerated phase-down of hydrofluorocarbons, which could reduce cooling-related emissions by up to 97 per cent. In response, the subprogramme has supported the advancement of a global agenda, including convening 72 Member States under the Global Cooling Pledge, backed by the newly launched Enabling Pledge Implementation for Cooling Facility to provide technical assistance and funding for heat action plans and pilots, and initiating an Intergovernmental Committee on Cooling, launched at the 2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference. The Intergovernmental Committee on Cooling monitors and accelerates the 14 targets of the Global Cooling Pledge. Since 2025, the subprogramme has been providing national and subnational signatories of the Global Cooling Pledge access to financial support for pilot deployment, technical guidance, policy support, capacity-building and feasibility assessments through the Enabling Pledge Implementation for Cooling Facility.

Result 3: governments at the national and subnational levels adopt climate adaptation strategies and policies, including national adaptation plans – Proposed programme plan for 2027

14.25	In its Adaptation Gap Report 2025, entitled Running on Empty: The World Is Gearing Up for Climate Resilience – Without the Money to Get There, UNEP updated the estimated adaptation finance needs to be $310 billion annually by 2035; meanwhile, at its thirtieth session, held in Brazil, the Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change emphasized robust adaptation plans and measurement, including the launch of the Belém-Addis vision on adaptation, a two-year policy alignment process by Parties, aimed at developing guidance for operationalizing the Belém Adaptation Indicators under the Belém-Addis vision on adaptation. The subprogramme provides support through a range of activities that help Member States in adopting adaptation policies and strategies. UNEP implements the Global Ecosystem-based Adaptation Fund, hosts the secretariat of the Global Adaptation Network, co-leads the World Adaptation Science Programme and effectively executes a wide portfolio of projects financed by the Global Environmental Facility, the Green Climate Fund and the Adaptation Fund.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.26	The lesson for the subprogramme was that its support for adaptation interventions was effective when the interventions were locally owned and supported by inclusive, multi-stakeholder partnerships and when there was financing available for implementation. In applying these lessons, the subprogramme will strengthen institutional capacities to lead adaptation efforts nationally and locally, replicate proven infrastructure models, increase private co-financing and enhance the availability of climate adaptation data to better support governments at different levels in developing and adopting climate adaptation strategies and policies that reflect their individual circumstances.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Digital transformations in supporting environmental action
Objective

14.29	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the use of data and analytics on environmental issues to allow more effective policies, decisions, actions and investments by Member States and the private sector, supporting multilateral environmental agreements and Sustainable Development Goal targets related to climate, nature, land, oceans and freshwater bodies and pollution.
Strategy
14.30	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Develop and maintain interoperable data systems, standards and analytical tools to support Sustainable Development Goal monitoring and the tracking of environmental impacts and compliance;

(b)	Provide digital platforms and technical support services to Member States, related to access to, the integration of and the use of environmental data and evidence for policy development, planning and decision-making;

(c)	Build digital capacity for environmental policy, planning and action, including through inclusive approaches, strengthened digital literacy, the integration of digital tools into national development planning and enhanced regional cooperation, particularly in developing countries, including the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States;

(d)	Provide technical guidance, advisory services and capacity-building to Member States and partners on the responsible use of artificial intelligence and other digital technologies for environmental policy and action, including an understanding of opportunities, risks and governance approaches;
(e)	Develop and disseminate shared principles, standards and good practices to promote environmentally sustainable and risk-informed digital innovation.
14.31	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 9, 10, 16 and 17.
14.32	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Timely, evidence-based decision-making on environmental issues at the national and global levels;

(b)	Policy coherence and improved accountability of Member States for progress towards internationally agreed environmental and development targets, including the Sustainable Development Goals and multilateral environmental agreements;

(c)	Data-driven environmental policies, plans and investments, particularly in developing countries, including the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States, contributing to reduced digital divides and enhanced regional and cross-border cooperation;

(d)	Increased adoption and responsible use of artificial intelligence and other emerging digital technologies for environmental applications, guided by sustainability principles and best practices that support innovation, while managing environmental, social and economic risks.
Programme performance in 2025
Governments and policymakers gained access to an artificial intelligence-powered environment portal

14.33	In 2025, the subprogramme launched EnvironmentGPT, an artificial intelligence-powered digital assistant designed to improve access to trusted, policy-relevant environmental knowledge. Developed as part of UNEP digital transformation efforts, it builds on prior investments in interoperable data governance approaches, with a focus on environmental data, knowledge management and responsible artificial intelligence. The tool enables users to query UNEP-curated environmental content through natural language, returning structured, cited responses drawn from authoritative UNEP publications and data sources. The public beta launch at the seventh session of the United Nations Environment Assembly marked a milestone in operationalizing artificial intelligence to strengthen the science-policy interface and demonstrated a shift from static knowledge repositories towards a more interactive and user-centred access model for environmental information.

In recent years, the subprogramme has worked to improve the availability of digital environmental resources, digital platforms, interoperable data governance approaches and artificial intelligence-enabled information access.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.40	The lesson for the subprogramme was that consolidating digital solutions and data sets within a unified platform could enhance their visibility, discoverability and early uptake by intended users. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place greater emphasis on integrating digital solutions and data sets across UNEP thematic areas within the World Environment Situation Room, with increased attention paid to initial uptake and use by countries and UNEP-supported stakeholders. This is expected to improve accessibility, coherence and initial uptake of environmental information by countries and UNEP stakeholders.

Subprogramme 3 – Nature action
Objective

14.43	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance actions to halt and reverse biodiversity loss; address desertification, land degradation and drought; and conserve and restore terrestrial, marine and freshwater ecosystems.
Strategy
14.44	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Enhance the capacity of Member States to sustainably manage and restore their natural and productive ecosystems;
(b)	Strengthen the capacity of Member States to integrate the sustainable management of nature into development planning and other cross-sectoral decision-making frameworks;

(c)	Promote a people-oriented approach to nature conservation, restoration and sustainable use, recognizing the importance of policies that support poverty eradication and the needs and livelihoods of biodiversity-dependent communities;
(d)	Promote a whole-of-government and whole-of-society approach to addressing interrelated nature conservation and development challenges.
14.45	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 3, 6, 12, 13, 14 and 15.
14.46	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Effective integration of sustainable management of biodiversity, land, freshwater bodies and oceans across sectors and systems;
(b)	Increased extent of landscapes and seascapes under improved ecosystem management;
(c)	Strengthened transparency, accountability and governance to deliver on biodiversity-, land-, freshwater- and ocean-related commitments;
(d)	Greater public and private financial flows directed towards sustainable ecosystem management and restoration.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States commit to protecting marine biodiversity beyond national borders

14.47	The subprogramme has supported Member States in advancing the Agreement under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea on the Conservation and Sustainable Use of Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction, adopted in June 2023, to safeguard nearly two thirds of the ocean that lies beyond national jurisdiction. Through its national-level enabling activities initiative, the subprogramme provided guidance, training and readiness support to 28 countries, establishing enabling conditions for the ratification and implementation of the Agreement. This support was designed to benefit countries worldwide, with a particular focus on developing countries and small island developing States that often face capacity constraints in ocean governance. Key beneficiaries include national ministries, regional organizations and local communities.

Result 3: expansion of sustainably managed and restored ecosystems – Proposed programme plan for 2027

14.53	Sustainable ecosystem management can help protect biodiversity and help maintain clean and healthy fresh and coastal waters and fertile land. Through its sustainable ecosystem management initiatives, the subprogramme provides science-based policy guidance and supports the development of legal frameworks that strengthen governance and resource conservation. By offering technical tools, capacity-building and specialized expertise, the subprogramme empowers national ministries, regional organizations and local communities alike. By focusing on these beneficiaries, the subprogramme ensures that ecosystem management not only helps conserve biodiversity and restore degraded environments, but also reinforces institutional capacity and community resilience.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.54	The lesson for the subprogramme was that tailoring capacity-building support to the diverse institutional and technical contexts of beneficiary countries enhanced relevance, ownership and effectiveness and that flexible, demand-driven and modular approaches were better suited to responding to varying governance structures, resources and priorities. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen its approach by delivering technically robust and context-responsive support that empowers beneficiaries to apply ecosystem management tools effectively within their own institutional and environmental settings.

Subprogramme 4 – Environmental law and governance
Objective

14.57	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure that countries achieve environmental policy coherence and abide by strong legal and institutional frameworks that increasingly implement environmental goals in the context of sustainable development at the global, regional and national levels.
Strategy
14.58	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Facilitate relevant meetings and intergovernmental processes at the global, regional and national levels involving Governments, other United Nations entities, inter-agency mechanisms and regional ministerial forums through information management products, technical support and advisory services on environmental law and policy;

(b)	Assist with the coherent implementation of multilateral environmental agreements by providing support to strengthen international and national efforts to achieve global environmental targets and by supporting Parties in national reporting to related conventions;

(c)	Develop and disseminate innovative knowledge management tools and programmatic initiatives such as the United Nations Information Portal on Multilateral Environmental Agreements and the Programme for the Development and Periodic Review of Environmental Law (Montevideo Programme), supported by training, technical assistance and communication activities, including mainstreaming a gender perspective;

(d)	Promote the uptake of environmentally sound practices across the United Nations system through the provision of policy advice and cooperate with United Nations country teams to mainstream lessons learned through the implementation of programmes, including replicating and scaling up interventions.
14.59	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 5, 12, 13, 15, 16 and 17.
14.60	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Increased efficiency and effectiveness of environmental law and policymaking processes leading to coherent and science-based policies, including through Member States’ concerted action on international environmental issues;

(b)	Improved governance and partnerships with major groups and stakeholders in the development of synergistic national programmes, as well as increased integration of environmental considerations into national and subnational planning and budgeting processes on sustainable development.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States advance legal frameworks and national strategies to address illegal waste trafficking

14.61	Illegal trafficking of waste remains a growing transboundary challenge in the Asia-Pacific region, with evolving waste trade flows and increasing volumes of plastic and hazardous waste placing pressure on enforcement and regulatory systems. UNEP, in partnership with the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC), contributed to Member State representatives of environmental authorities, customs administrations and trade and industry ministries across the region enhancing their technical and operational capacities to detect, prevent and respond to illegal waste trafficking through strengthened regional cooperation, policy dialogue and knowledge exchange mechanisms. The subprogramme, which has the responsibility for advancing legal frameworks and national strategies to address illegal waste trafficking, supported a UNODC-UNEP regional technical seminar on waste trafficking and the circular economy approaches held in Kuala Lumpur. The subprogramme also held regional meetings in Bangkok and Jakarta to facilitate policy dialogue between the European Union and Association of Southeast Asian Nations member States, including Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and Viet Nam, on waste trafficking trends and related regulatory frameworks.

14.62	As a result, Member State representatives improved their understanding of waste trafficking trends and enforcement challenges, advanced the development of legal frameworks and strategies to address illegal waste trafficking, including linkages to organized crime and trade systems, and strengthened alignment with regional circular economy approaches and national action planning processes.

Result 3: environmental objectives are integrated into national policy and legal frameworks – Proposed programme plan for 2027

14.68	The integration of environmental objectives into national policy and legal frameworks help with the translation of internationally agreed environmental commitments into coherent national action. Through its work with Governments and relevant institutions, the subprogramme has supported the incorporation of environmental objectives into national policy and legal frameworks, contributing to improved coherence across sectors and stronger alignment between national development processes and internationally agreed environmental objectives.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.69	The lesson for the subprogramme was that the sustained integration of environmental objectives could be augmented when support was provided at key stages of policy and legal development, rather than after instruments had already been finalized. Experience showed that clearer articulation of environmental objectives within broader policy frameworks, and their linkage to planning, monitoring and budgetary processes, strengthened ownership and durability. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place greater emphasis on timely and targeted technical and advisory support to assist in the integration of environmental objectives during the formulation and revision of national policy and legal frameworks, with a focus on strengthening cross-sectoral coherence and consistency with internationally agreed environmental objectives.

Subprogramme 5 – Chemicals and pollution action
Objective

14.72	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to transition to a pollution-free planet, with a focus on reducing pollution in air, soil and water and thus ensure environmental quality and health for people and the planet.
Strategy
14.73	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide technical advice and capacity-building to Member States, the private sector and civil society, within the framework of relevant multilateral environmental agreements, the Global Framework on Chemicals – for a Planet Free of Harm from Chemicals and Waste and the broader international chemicals and waste agenda, with a view to strengthening legal, policy and regulatory frameworks and promoting the uptake of circular economy approaches and sustainable consumption and production approaches in key sectors and value chains;
(b)	Promote the use of credible science and data on pollution and its sources, including through the new Intergovernmental Science-Policy Panel on Chemicals, Waste and Pollution;

(c)	Collaborate with partners from the environment, health and other relevant sectors and support countries’ efforts in addressing issues at the interface of environment and health, in order to achieve environmental and health benefits, including through actions to improve air quality and to address the environmental dimensions of antimicrobial resistance and other related issues, including through a One Health approach.
14.74	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 3, 6, 11, 12 and 14.
14.75	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	A reduction in exposure to harmful chemicals and pollution;

(b)	Countries developing, adopting, revising or implementing policies, laws or regulations that advance circular economy approaches, sound management for chemicals and waste and pollution prevention and control in key sectors;
(c)	Private sector entities taking action to improve chemicals and waste management and reduce pollution;
(d)	Sectoral and cross-sectoral efforts advancing positive outcomes for the environment and health.
Programme performance in 2025
Chemicals of global concern and their waste reduced

14.76	Hazardous chemicals such as mercury, highly hazardous pesticides, persistent organic pollutants and their waste pose serious risks to human health and the environment. The subprogramme, in partnership with the Global Environment Facility, has worked with governments, other organizations and the private sector to cut emissions and safely dispose of chemicals of concern and their waste, including those covered by multilateral environmental agreements. UNEP has been supporting more environmentally sound practices and sustainable solutions, supported by targeted training and technical assistance in sectors where regulated chemicals are used. UNEP has also been supporting the strengthening of national and regional institutions to implement priority chemicals and waste interventions, including through the establishment of chemical observatories.
14.77	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 14.XIII).

Planned results for 2027
Result 1: adoption of policies and practices to support circular economy approaches in high-impact sectors to reduce pollution – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

14.78	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the development and implementation of the Framework Act on the Prevention and Sustainable Management of Waste in the Comoros and the development of a road map for mandatory plastic extended producer responsibility and guidelines for the recovery and recycling of plastic waste in Zimbabwe, for a total of five governments, including subnational governments, and producer responsibility organizations taking policy action to advance extended producer responsibility and similar scheme development to prevent and reduce pollution, which met the planned target.

Result 2: adoption of policies and strategies to address the environmental dimensions of antimicrobial resistance – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

14.80	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the development of a legal framework on antimicrobial resistance in Peru and a series of other initiatives, such as a UNEP-led workshop on the environmental dimensions of antimicrobial resistance in Guatemala, policy guidance, through its contribution to the advisory board of the Climate Change Impacts on Antimicrobial Resistance Using a Planetary Health Framework network, and tools that include the One Health Legislative Assessment Tool for Antimicrobial Resistance, school-based antimicrobial resistance education and the Quadripartite antimicrobial resistance toolkit series for young people, educators and policymakers, for a total of eight initiatives, policies, strategies or action plans undertaken by public or private actors from key economic sectors to address the environmental factors driving antimicrobial resistance, which met the planned target.

14.82	The subprogramme continues to support the strengthening of capacities and legal, policy and regulatory frameworks for the sound management of chemicals and waste, including through the implementation of the Global Framework on Chemicals to enhance the sound management of chemicals and waste and by helping countries meet their commitments under conventions on chemicals and waste. UNEP provides secretariat services to the newly established Intergovernmental Science-Policy Panel on Chemicals, Waste and Pollution.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.83	The lesson for the subprogramme was that anchoring its interventions in policy and regulatory action helped ensure durable progress in chemicals and waste management and pollution reduction. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support countries in strengthening their legal, policy and regulatory frameworks for the sound management of chemicals and waste in key sectors such as plastics, textiles, minerals and metals, electronics transport and buildings and construction.

children.text: Subprogramme 6 – Finance and economic transformations
Objective

14.86	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure that countries and businesses integrate sustainability into economic, finance, legal and regulatory frameworks and catalyse public and private finance for sustainable consumption and production, circular economy approaches and business models in high-impact sectors.
Strategy
14.87	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support national and subnational governments in the design and implementation of policies and initiatives for sustainable consumption and production and circular economy approaches that reduce greenhouse gas emissions and pollutants and help restore ecosystem functions;

(b)	Catalyse private sector investment for sustainability by engaging financial institutions and other private sector actors to align investment and lending with national development priorities, including support for small and medium-sized enterprises;
(c)	Leverage public budgets and fiscal policies to mobilize private finance by advising Member States on public and fiscal measures that catalyse sustainable investment;

(d)	Develop and refine metrics to track stocks and flows of natural and other forms of capital, by supporting the development and application of indicators that inform environmental, economic and financial decision-making;

(e)	Support national and subnational governments in building institutional capacities and implementing development plans and policies and support businesses in adopting sustainable management and cleaner production practices across value chains.
14.88	These workstreams will help Member States make progress towards the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 4, 8, 12, 13, 15 and 17.
14.89	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Governments and public and private sector institutions adopting or updating policy instruments and/or standards that accelerate sustainable consumption and production, including circular economy approaches and the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication;

(b)	Governments implementing fiscal, budgetary and policy measures that unlock or redirect public and private finance towards sustainable investments aligned with climate-, biodiversity- and pollution-related objectives and objectives relating to circular economy approaches;

(c)	Banks and insurance institutions adopting governance arrangements, strategies, targets or disclosures to manage climate-, nature- and pollution-related risks and opportunities and reduce environmental footprints;
(d)	Increased use of UNEP-supported knowledge, data, metrics and assessments to inform decision-making and strengthen evidence-based policymaking.
Programme performance in 2025
Insurance companies commit to sustainability approaches in their business practices

14.90	The Principles for Sustainable Insurance provide a global framework for the insurance industry to address environmental, social and governance risks and opportunities. The subprogramme works with insurers on incorporating elements of the Principles, including biodiversity-related risks consistent with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. In parallel, support was provided to insurers for the development of market-based insurance solutions tailored to economies exposed to the adverse effects of climate change, improving access to insurance for small and medium-sized enterprises and strengthening financial resilience in the face of the adverse impacts of climate change. The subprogramme launched a report on how insurers can undertake nature-related dependency, impact, risk and opportunity assessments and, together with the Brazilian Insurance Confederation, held a global sustainable insurance summit at the 2025 United Nations Climate Change Conference, to reinforce the industry’s role in contributing to a just, orderly and equitable transition away from fossil fuels in energy systems.
14.91	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 14.VII).
Figure 14.XVII
Performance measure: number of insurance companies that adopted the Principles for Sustainable Insurance (cumulative)
Planned results for 2027

Result 1: increased availability of tools and measures to transform choices and behaviour by governments, businesses and individual consumers for environmental sustainability – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

14.92	The subprogramme’s work contributed to an additional 17 consumer information and education tools and products that inform decision-making, choices and changes in behaviour towards sustainability, including digital product information systems, tools to improve product sustainability information, the UNEP Finance Initiative Portfolio Impact Analysis Tool for Banks and the EnABLE toolkit for sustainable infrastructure, for a total of 366 tools and products, which exceeded the planned target of 361.
14.93	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 14.XVIII).
Figure 14.XVIII
Performance measure: number of consumer information and education tools and products that inform decision-making, choices and changes in behaviour towards sustainability (cumulative)
Result 2: accelerating progress towards sustainable consumption and production patterns – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

14.94	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 187 initiatives taken by Member States in integrating sustainable consumption and production and economic and financial incentives into national planning, including a carbon label-based supply chain management initiative in China, the integration by Indonesia of sustainable consumption and production indicators and fiscal incentives into its development plan and a road map for sustainable finance and the circular economy in Kyrgyzstan, which exceeded the planned target of 186.
14.95	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 14.XIX).
Figure 14.XIX

Performance measure: number of initiatives undertaken by Member States to integrate sustainable consumption and production and economic and financial incentives into national planning and/or sectoral strategies (cumulative)
Result 3: Member States adopt policies to increase sustainable finance – Proposed programme plan for 2027

14.96	Government policy action can help facilitate the availability of public and private finance for investments with sustainability objectives. This subprogramme supports national and subnational governments, in collaboration with partners, in integrating sustainability into economic, fiscal and investment decision-making. Support is focused on budget formulation, fiscal reforms, public investment and incentive frameworks that influence the scale, direction and quality of finance in high-impact sectors, contributing to improved policy coherence and the alignment of public finance with environmental priorities, consistent with the Paris Agreement and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.97	The lesson for the subprogramme was that incentives for financing a clean healthy and sustainable environment could be strengthened when fiscal and investment analysis was embedded in national budget cycles and aligned with country priorities. Sustained interministerial engagement, alignment with fiscal decision points and country ownership supported the translation of analysis into policy action. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will support Member States in integrating policy packages that promote sustainable investments into budget preparation and expenditure reviews, with a specific focus on fiscal and investment instruments that have clear implementation pathways.
14.98	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 14.XX).
Figure 14.XX

Subprogramme 7 – Science-policy
Objective

14.100	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance the generation and utilization of quality environmental data and analyses by Governments and non-governmental actors, enabling a science-policy interface with a view to keeping the environment under review, building science-based environmental knowledge, identifying emerging issues and developing science-based policy globally, regionally and nationally, including for the achievement of the Goals.
Strategy
14.101	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Review the world environmental situation and strengthen the interface between science and policymaking across the sustainable development agenda, including through collaboration with the Statistical Commission and the five regional commissions;

(b)	Develop scientifically credible and unbiased data, information and knowledge and provide policy-relevant analysis and policy recommendations to catalyse and accelerate solutions and action for the environment, based on a continuous review of the global status of the environment and trends;

(c)	Catalyse systematic and transformative shifts that can drive progress towards the aspirations of achieving the environmental dimension of the 2030 Agenda, through gender-sensitive partnerships across all geographical areas, involving Governments and non-governmental organizations, the business and scientific communities, and citizens;

(d)	Pursue broad stakeholder engagement as a critical step in enhancing societal understanding, acceptance and uptake of its scientific analysis and serve as a knowledge broker that builds consensus on existing and emerging environmental issues through platforms and networks, such as the Science-Policy-Business Forum on the Environment, the International Resource Panel, the World Adaptation Science Programme and the One Planet network;

(e)	Provide analytical research, increase awareness about the environmental dimension of sustainable development and build capacity to measure, monitor and report on progress, helping Member States integrate common methods for tracking and measuring the environment-related Sustainable Development Goals, in particular Goals 1, 2, 4, 6, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15 and 17, including in their national processes.
14.102	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased production and use of scientific environmental data by Member States and other stakeholders;
(b)	A more coherent approach to harmonizing country data, to tracking progress and to science-based environmental policymaking, within and among countries;

(c)	Member States improving their tracking and comparability of progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals, which enhances coherence for the integration of national sustainable development plans and other internationally agreed environmental goals.
Programme performance in 2025
Countries implement the System of Environmental-Economic Accounting

14.103	UNEP has continuously supported national and regional capacities to generate, harmonize and use environment-related statistics in decision-making. One of the areas where the subprogramme has provided dedicated support to Member States is in the implementation of the System of Environmental-Economic Accounting. As at December 2025, UNEP has successfully co-conducted six targeted capacity-building events, including regional workshops, seminars and national technical training sessions on the System. The events covered 12 countries from the Asia-Pacific, Latin America and the Caribbean and Africa regions. UNEP also contributed to the global e-learning course on measuring material flows in the Sustainable Development Goals, organized by the Statistical Institute for Asia and the Pacific of the Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific. These efforts were built on previous work and ultimately contributed to 98 countries successfully putting in place functional systems for environmental-economic accounting, of which 71 are being implemented at the tier 3 level, meaning that they have institutionalized their compilation methodologies and are now compiling data on their environmental statistical accounts on a regular basis.

Result 3: Governments and institutions use United Nations Environment Programme knowledge, data, statistics and assessments to inform policymaking – Proposed programme plan for 2027

14.109	The subprogramme organizes regional ministerial forums, including the Forum of Ministers and Environment Authorities of Asia-Pacific, the African Ministerial Conference on the Environment and the Forum of Ministers of Environment of Latin America and Caribbean. The goal of these forums is to seek commitments to science-policy integration for tackling climate change, biodiversity loss and pollution. The subprogramme combines these in-person convenings with online modalities leveraging UNEP resources, to ensure the full integration of technical understanding following the in-person engagement. A key component of this practice has been virtual learning, which has broadened access and ensured that practical application, targeted follow-up and in-country technical assistance was maintained following in-person engagements.
Lessons learned and planned change

14.110	The lesson for the subprogramme was that progress in translating the commitments of the in-person convenings to policymaking and action towards sustainability often hinged on institutional capacity, the continuity of trained personnel and robust interoperable data governance approaches across national agencies. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will prioritize strengthening capacity-building using UNEP knowledge, data, statistics and assessments. The subprogramme plans to intensify post-training coaching and the formal designation of data custodianship in national institutions to enhance reporting continuity, data quality and the seamless integration of scientific evidence into policy and planning. This modality will inform 12 or more capacity-building initiatives in 2026 and 2027, equipping over 500 policymakers with skills for evidence-based decisions, including the outcomes of the seventh session of the United Nations Environment Assembly.
